previewTitle.value: A Health report on the Amazon - Perpetual Planet

title.value: A Health report on the Amazon

text.value:
Rolex is supporting its Perpetual Planet initiative partner, the National Geographic Society, as it embarks on a critical, two-year study of the Amazon River Basin.

A series of expeditions, from the Andes to the Atlantic, will illuminate the region’s health status and provide solutions to protect the world’s largest single rainforest for future generations.

With its mantle of tall trees, its riot of plants and animals, along with its sheer size, the Amazon rainforest looms large in the collective imagination as a force of nature that will always prevail. But every year this primordial landscape, extending across eight countries and inhabited by some 40 million people, is assailed by increasing deforestation that threatens the hydrological processes that have created and sustained it.

The Amazon rainforest is one of Earth’s most critical ecosystems, breathing out oxygen and with it the moisture that seeds rain. It is not just the region’s estimated 3 million or so species of plants and animals that are impressive, but also the amount of water that courses through the basin. Its rivers channel the largest volume of rainfall on Earth. This mighty river system, with its 1,100 tributaries, can be considered the lifeblood of our planet.

The health of that system is the focus of the third major Perpetual Planet initiative venture since Rolex enhanced its long-standing partnership with the National Geographic Society to carry out a series of expeditions answering critical questions about the impacts of climate change on vital environments.
Over the next two years, Rolex will support a series of expeditions led by National Geographic Explorers, scientists, storytellers and local community members.

The first expedition, to Everest in 2019, was to improve understanding of the effects of climate change on the glaciers of the Hindu Kush-Himalaya. Climate scientists Baker Perry and Tom Matthews and a team of Sherpas also installed the world’s highest weather station on the mountain’s south-east ridge. This was followed by an expedition in 2021 – led by Perry – to install the highest weather station in the Southern and Western hemispheres, just below the summit of Tupungato Volcano in the Southern Andes.

Over the next two years, Rolex will support a series of expeditions led by National Geographic Explorers, scientists, storytellers and local community members as they investigate the Amazon basin, showcasing its intricate connectivity. They will hone in on the region’s hydrological cycle and the critical role that the river’s seasonal flooding has on access to fresh water and the survival of communities and wildlife. This work will build on the Tropical Forest Vulnerability Index developed by National Geographic in 2019, with the assistance of Rolex. The Index indicates that each forest reacts differently to stressors such as heat, drought, fires and pollution, and requires a diversity of solutions.

For all its vastness, the Amazon rainforest is vulnerable. As fragmentation occurs from new roads or deforestation from fires turbocharged by climate change, affected areas become drier and rainfall decreases markedly, setting off what scientists call “dieback”, a self-perpetuating loop of destruction, which could see huge swathes of forest turn into savannah.

“The Amazon is one of the most complex and essential environments in the world and it is increasingly at risk of losing its most valuable resource: water,” says João Campos-Silva, a 2019 Laureate of the Rolex Awards for Enterprise and a National Geographic Explorer. “It’s deeply important to explore this environment while partnering with Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IPLCs) so that we can have a holistic understanding of what those communities need and the steps we can all take to better protect the Amazon.”
The expeditions are being led by National Geographic Explorers:

legendRoller.value: João Campos-Silva will join Andressa Scabin to investigate aquatic wildlife throughout the Amazon basin and partner with local communities to design the first region-wide, community-based conservation model.

Baker Perry and Tom Matthews will place a weather station near the peak of Nevado Ausangate, one of the highest points in the Amazon watershed, and monitor climate change impacts on the water tower, the primary freshwater source for Andean and downstream ecosystems.

Angelo Bernardino and Margaret Owuor will explore mangroves at the mouth of the Amazon River to assess changes in carbon sequestration. They will also conduct the first-ever mapping assessment of ecosystem services in this region.

Ruthmery Pillco Huarcaya and Andy Whitworth will research the impacts of deforestation and climate change on the Andean bear, whose life cycle and migratory range support the ecosystem of the cloud forest (elevated rainforest).

Fernando Trujillo will track pink river dolphins along the Amazon and assess the level of mercury contamination in their diet as an indication of the waterway’s health. He will also partner with the local community to develop fishing agreements and tree-planting initiatives to prevent run-off.

Hinsby Cadillo-Quiroz and Josh West will be joined by geologist Jennifer Angel Amaya to explore two rivers to determine the impact of deforestation and mining on water quality. This will mark the first evaluation of carbon and mercury production in mining ponds, and their subsequent impact on water flow paths.

Thiago Silva will study the carbon and nutrient contents in the soil and leaf morphology of aquatic forests to provide a barometer for the capacity of the Amazon rainforest to absorb greenhouse gases.

text.value: To document the Amazon River, National Geographic Photographer and Explorer Thomas Peschak will be immersed with the Explorers, travelling from its source amongst snow-capped Andean volcanoes to Brazilian mangrove forests. He will also dive below the water’s surface to reveal a rarely glimpsed aquatic underworld. His storytelling will spotlight the threats and showcase the solutions, science and communities working to secure the future of the Amazon.

pageTitle.value: Mt Everest sensors give new insights into climate change

pageCaption.value: In May 2019, as part of its Perpetual Planet partnership with Rolex, National Geographic succeeded in leading an expedition to install the world’s highest weather station on Mt Everest.

WaPageTitle.value: Mt Everest sensors give new insights into climate change

metadescription.value: Discover how, as part of Perpetual Planet, Rolex and National Geographic mounted a successful expedition to install the world’s highest weather station.

previewTitle.value: Mt Everest sensors give new insights into climate change

pageTitle.value: Perpetual Planet - An expedition to the Yucatán cave systems

pageCaption.value: Pollution from cities and tourism is contaminating the aquifer that is Yucatán’s sole source of fresh water. Six of the world’s best cave divers spent two weeks on a Perpetual Planet-supported expedition mapping cave systems and taking water samples from sinkholes that were explored for the first time in the modern era.

WaPageTitle.value: Yucatán, Mexico — A giant aquifer under siege

metadescription.value: Robbie Schmittner led a scientific expedition to the caves of Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula to find a solution to water pollution. More on Rolex.org.
